  TaskCreate, mark #1-#5 as completed, and continue from task #6, but only after the VM deploy described in "The ONE thing still left on this slice" is verified green.

1	# petboard — continuation handoff
2	
3	You are picking up a petboard session that was started on commonlisp6's
4	mac. This directory holds everything you need to continue from where
5	the previous agent left off.
6	
7	## Read this first
8	
9	Before doing anything else, read the full transcript of the prior
10	session:
11	
12	```
13	services/petboard/handoff/session.jsonl
14	```
15	
16	It is a Claude Code session jsonl. Each line is a JSON object; you
17	want the `message` blocks (user and assistant) to reconstruct the
18	conversation. The file is ~2 MB and contains the design discussion,
19	every tool call, and every decision made so far. **Read it in full
20	before touching any files** — it encodes roughly two hours of
21	back-and-forth design work you will not be able to rediscover from
22	the code alone.
23	
24	After the transcript, read:
25	
26	- `services/petboard/PLAN.md` — full design doc (schema, canvas UX,
27	  auth architecture, install layout, implementation order, open risks)
28	- `services/petboard/README.md` — one-paragraph pointer to PLAN
29	- `services/petboard/server/db/seed.sql` — the bootstrap data that
30	  plants petboard as its own first project on first init
31	
32	## Current state
33	
34	**Committed and pushed to origin/main:**
35	
36	- `a3a0fde` "Add petboard service scaffold and backend v1"
37	  - `services/petboard/` full tree (Go backend, React scaffold, PLAN,
38	    seed, caddy snippet, install/uninstall scripts)
39	- A later commit adding this handoff directory and a PATH fix to
40	  `services/install-petboard.sh`.
41	
42	**Inside the `3d347fa` commit (already on origin) alive-server has
43	three changes relevant to petboard:**
44	
45	1. A public-path registry that reads `/etc/attlas-public-paths.d/*.conf`
46	   on startup and on SIGHUP, and short-circuits `handleAuthVerify` to
47	   return 200 for any request whose `X-Forwarded-Uri` starts with a
48	   registered prefix.
49	2. Return-URL preservation across the Google OAuth round-trip. The old
50	   behavior always redirected to `/` after login, losing the original
51	   URL and all its query params. Now `handleAuthVerify` passes the
52	   original URI as `return_to=`, the state store carries it through to
53	   `handleOAuth2Callback`, and the callback redirects to the captured
54	   URL (validated as a same-origin relative path via
55	   `isSafeRelativePath`).
56	3. An `os/signal` + `syscall` SIGHUP handler that triggers
57	   `publicPathRegistry.load()` so services can reload without dropping
58	   sessions: `systemctl kill --signal=SIGHUP alive-server`.
59	
60	These were tested only at the source level (grep + code review). The
61	alive-server binary on the VM has ALREADY been rebuilt and restarted
62	during this session (see session.jsonl around the `go build` +
63	`systemctl restart alive-server` calls). Confirm with
64	`systemctl status alive-server` — the journalctl should show a start
65	time of ~19:41 UTC on Apr 11 2026 or later.
66	
67	## Task tracker
68	
69	14 tasks live in the Claude task system. State as of handoff:
70	
71	| id | status | subject |
72	|----|--------|---------|
73	| 1 | completed | Probe Caddy forward_auth for X-Forwarded-Uri |
74	| 2 | completed | alive-server public-path registry + return-URL preservation |
75	| 3 | completed | Scaffold petboard directory tree |
76	| 4 | completed | Backend v1 (CRUD + seed loader) — verified locally |
77	| 5 | completed | Install script, systemd unit, Caddy snippet |
78	| 6 | pending | Frontend scaffold (fetch /api/projects, render list) |
79	| 7 | pending | Project detail page |
80	| 8 | pending | Canvas v1 (react-konva threads/orbs/semantic zoom) |
81	| 9 | pending | Canvas v2 (drag, filters, time window, polish) |
82	| 10 | pending | SSE live updates from server to canvas |
83	| 11 | pending | OAuth 2.1 endpoints (well-known, DCR, authorize, token) |
84	| 12 | pending | MCP endpoint and tool surface |
85	| 13 | pending | Claude Code skill + end-to-end test |
86	| 14 | pending | Polish (errors, empty states, shortcuts) |
87	
88	The task list on the VM side will NOT have these pre-populated. You
89	should re-create them via `TaskCreate` using this table as the source
90	of truth.
91	
92	## Verified-works slice
93	
94	The backend was built and fully exercised on the mac locally before
95	handoff. Every piece below was confirmed working end-to-end via curl
96	at `http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/...`:
97	
98	- `go build` on `server/` produces a 14 MB binary
99	- `npm run build` on `web/` produces a ~143 KB bundle
100	- Server startup applies migration `0001_init.sql` cleanly
101	- On an empty DB the bootstrap seed runs and plants petboard-as-first-project
102	- `GET /petboard/` serves the React scaffold HTML with `/petboard/assets/*` paths rewritten by Vite's base
103	- `GET /petboard/api/projects` returns petboard with aggregates `feature_counts={backlog:7, done:1}` and `total_minutes=120`
104	- `GET /petboard/api/projects/petboard` returns 8 features + 1 effort log row
105	- `POST /api/projects` with no `problem` → 400 "invalid input: problem is required" (validation fires)
106	- `POST /api/projects` with valid payload → 201, slug + color derived
107	- `POST .../features` + `PATCH /api/features/{id}` with `status=in_progress` → auto-sets `started_at`
108	- `POST .../effort` → effort log row + aggregate update on re-fetch
109	- SPA prefix stripping and static asset MIME types correct
110	
111	The local iteration loop used `/tmp/petboard` as the binary and
112	`/tmp/petboard-dev.db` as the DB. Both were cleaned up before the
113	handoff commit.
114	
115	## The ONE thing still left on this slice: deploy to the VM
116	
117	The user explicitly asked for local-first iteration but also said
118	"make sure to deploy and test in the end after we are happy with the
119	local dev setup". The local dev is working. The next step on this
120	slice is to run the install script on the VM and confirm petboard
121	comes up through `https://attlas.uk/petboard/`.
122	
123	### What broke on the first VM deploy attempt (already fixed in commit)
124	
125	Earlier in the session I ran the install script on the VM and it got
126	as far as `npm run build` (success) then died on:
127	
128	```
129	Building petboard Go binary...
130	bash: line 2: go: command not found
131	```
132	
133	Root cause: `sudo -u agnostic-user -H bash -c "..."` runs a non-login
134	shell, so `/etc/profile.d/*` (where `go` is added to PATH by the
135	attlas base-setup) is never sourced. Fix that is now in the committed
136	install script: every `sudo -u` invocation goes through
137	`env PATH="/usr/local/go/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin" bash -c ...`
138	so the build environment is deterministic regardless of how the
139	operator's shell is configured.
140	
141	### How to finish the deploy
142	
143	```bash
144	# Pull (use agnostic-user since that's who owns the clone)
145	sudo -u agnostic-user git -C /home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas pull --ff-only
146	
147	# Run the install script. It will build the frontend + backend as
148	# agnostic-user, install the systemd unit, register public paths,
149	# SIGHUP alive-server, and drop the Caddy snippet.
150	sudo bash /home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas/services/install-petboard.sh
151	
152	# services/install.sh reloads Caddy at the end when run via the menu;
153	# when running install-petboard.sh directly, reload manually:
154	sudo systemctl reload caddy
155	```
156	
157	Verify:
158	
159	```bash
160	sudo journalctl -u petboard -n 30 --no-pager
161	sudo journalctl -u alive-server -n 20 --no-pager   # look for "public-paths: loaded 4 prefix(es)"
162	curl -sI https://attlas.uk/petboard/                # expect 302 to /oauth2/login?return_to=/petboard/
163	```
164	
165	Then open `https://attlas.uk/petboard/` in a browser — after Google
166	OAuth you should land on the petboard scaffold page (task #6 hasn't
167	been done yet, so it'll say "scaffold — the universe will live
168	here"), and `GET https://attlas.uk/petboard/api/projects` (with the
169	Caddy session cookie) should return the seeded data with petboard as
170	the first project.
171	
172	### Important: verify the return-URL preservation
173	
174	This is the single most load-bearing alive-server change and it has
175	only been tested at the source level. When you `curl -sI
176	https://attlas.uk/petboard/`, the Location header should be
177	`/oauth2/login?return_to=%2Fpetboard%2F` — not just `/oauth2/login`.
178	That's the proof the new handleAuthVerify code path is live. If it's
179	still plain `/oauth2/login`, alive-server either wasn't rebuilt or
180	wasn't restarted with the new binary.
181	
182	## After deploy
183	
184	Once the VM deploy is green, move into task #6. It is the smallest
185	frontend task that actually starts rendering server data:
186	
187	1. Add `react-router` with `basename="/petboard"` and two routes:
188	   `/` → `<Universe />`, `/p/:slug` → `<ProjectDetail />`.
189	2. Add `QueryClientProvider` around the app.
190	3. Build a tiny `api/client.ts` — a thin wrapper over `fetch` that
191	   prepends `/petboard/api` and throws on non-2xx responses.
192	4. In `Universe.tsx` fetch `/api/projects` via `useQuery` and render a
193	   plain unordered list: name, priority pill, progress ring ("7/12"),
194	   total hours. Ugly is fine, no canvas yet. This is the smoke test
195	   that proves the frontend ↔ backend round-trips through the real
196	   auth gate on the VM.
197	5. Same pattern for `ProjectDetail.tsx` → use task #7's content.
198	
199	Do NOT jump directly into the canvas (task #8). It is much more work
200	and debugging canvas + routing + auth simultaneously is miserable. Get
201	the plain list working first.
202	
203	## Rules I am holding you to
204	
205	These are saved as `feedback` memories. Follow them without being
206	reminded.
207	
208	1. **Never commit more than once per hour**, and only when deploying
209	   AND the deployment is verified working. Batch work across multiple
210	   tasks into a single commit at deploy-verify time. The only
211	   exception is an explicit user request to commit.
212	   (`feedback_commit_frequency.md`)
213	2. **Never add `Co-Authored-By: Claude`** to git commit messages.
214	   (`feedback_no_claude_coauthor.md`)
215	3. **Never tell the user to try something without testing it
216	   yourself first.** If you propose a command, run it.
217	   (`feedback_test_before_telling.md`)
218	4. **Commit and push every memory update to the dotfiles repo.** The
219	   memory directory is a symlink into `dotfiels/claude/memory` — any
220	   new or updated memory file needs a dotfiles commit + push.
221	   (`feedback_commit_memory_to_dotfiles.md`)
222	5. **Write an attlas diary entry when the user signals the session
223	   is done** — `attlas/diary/` contains Hugo markdown.
224	   (`feedback_diary.md`)
225	6. The user is **commonlisp6**. Never refer to them as pedro.
226	   (`user_identity.md`)
227	
228	## Useful paths
229	
230	| path | purpose |
231	|------|---------|
232	| `services/petboard/PLAN.md` | Full design doc |
233	| `services/petboard/server/` | Go backend (see `cmd/petboard/main.go`) |
234	| `services/petboard/web/` | Vite + React + Tailwind |
235	| `services/petboard/server/db/seed.sql` | Bootstrap data (petboard ↦ petboard) |
236	| `services/install-petboard.sh` | Installer (needs sudo) |
237	| `services/uninstall-petboard.sh` | Uninstaller (keeps /var/lib/petboard) |
238	| `base-setup/alive-server/main.go` | Has the public-path registry + return-URL fix |
239	| `/etc/attlas-public-paths.d/petboard.conf` | Registered public paths on the VM |
240	| `/var/lib/petboard/petboard.db` | Live sqlite DB on the VM |
241	| `/usr/local/share/petboard/dist` | Installed static asset directory |
242	
243	## Known gotchas
244	
245	- `go` is in `/usr/local/go/bin` on the VM, not on agnostic-user's
246	  non-login PATH. Install script now handles this explicitly.
247	- `alive-server` does not have an `ExecReload=` directive in its
248	  systemd unit. To trigger the public-path SIGHUP reloader, use
249	  `systemctl kill --signal=SIGHUP alive-server` (the install script
250	  does this automatically, falling back to `systemctl restart` on
251	  failure).
252	- The VM's attlas clone lives at `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas`.
253	  Git operations must be run as agnostic-user (`sudo -u`) because the
254	  working tree is owned by them.
255	- Caddy's `forward_auth` sets `X-Forwarded-Uri` on the subrequest to
256	  alive-server — this is what the public-path registry matches
257	  against. Confirmed via the Caddy docs during the probe.
258

three changes relevant to petboard:**

1. A public-path registry that reads `/etc/attlas-public-paths.d/*.conf`
   on startup and on SIGHUP, and short-circuits `handleAuthVerify` to

   registered prefix.
2. Return-URL preservation across the Google OAuth round-trip. The old
   behavior always redirected to `/` after login, losing the original
   URL and all its query params. Now `handleAuthVerify` passes the
   original URI as `return_to=`, the state store carries it through to
   `handleOAuth2Callback`, and the callback redirects to the captured

3. An `os/signal` + `syscall` SIGHUP handler that triggers

These were tested only at the source level (grep + code review). The

`systemctl restart alive-server` calls). Confirm with
`systemctl status alive-server` — the journalctl should show a start

## Task tracker

14 tasks live in the Claude task system. State as of handoff:

| id | status | subject |

| 2 | completed | alive-server public-path registry + return-URL preservation |

## Verified-works slice

The backend was built and fully exercised on the mac locally before

- `go build` on `server/` produces a 14 MB binary

Building petboard Go binary...
bash: line 2: go: command not found

This is the single most load-bearing alive-server change and it has

| `/etc/attlas-public-paths.d/petboard.conf` | Registered public paths on the VM |
| `/var/lib/petboard/petboard.db` | Live sqlite DB on the VM |
| `/usr/local/share/petboard/dist` | Installed static asset directory |

## Known gotchas

- `go` is in `/usr/local/go/bin` on the VM, not on agnostic-user's
  non-login PATH. Install script now handles this explicitly.
- `alive-server` does not have an `ExecReload=` directive in its
  systemd unit. To trigger the public-path SIGHUP reloader, use
  `systemctl kill --signal=SIGHUP alive-server` (the install script
  does this automatically, falling back to `systemctl restart` on
  failure).
- The VM's attlas clone lives at `/home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas`.
  Git operations must be run as agnostic-user (`sudo -u`) because the
  working tree is owned by them.
- Caddy's `forward_auth` sets `X-Forwarded-Uri` on the subrequest to
  alive-server — this is what the public-path registry matches
  against. Confirmed via the Caddy docs during the probe.

content.content:
# Attlas

Infrastructure and services monorepo for a single GCP VM environment.

## Directory Structure

```
attlas/
├── infra/              # Terraform — provisions VM, network, static IP
│   └── singlevm-setup/
├── base-setup/         # First-SSH setup — packages, dotfiles, Caddy gateway
└── services/           # Browser-accessible services — ttyd, code-server
```

## How It Works

1. **`infra/`** provisions a bare Ubuntu VM with `terraform apply`. Startup script clones this repo. Terraform also manages Secret Manager IAM bindings for all secrets.
2. **`base-setup/`** is run manually after first SSH. It installs packages, dotfiles, Node.js, Go, Claude Code, builds the Go dashboard server, installs Caddy, and auto-updates Cloudflare DNS. After completion, `https://attlas.uk/` serves the dashboard behind Google OAuth2.
3. **`services/`** installs browser-accessible services (cloud terminal, cloud VS Code, OpenClaw) and registers them with the Caddy gateway.

## IMPORTANT: Exposing Services to the Internet

Caddy is the single entry point for all HTTPS traffic. The base Caddyfile lives in `base-setup/Caddyfile` and imports route snippets from `/etc/caddy/conf.d/*.caddy`.

**To expose a new service to the internet, you MUST:**
1. Add a `.caddy` route snippet file to the `services/` directory
2. Copy it to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/` on the VM
3. Run `sudo systemctl reload caddy`

**Without a Caddy snippet, a service is only accessible on localhost and NOT reachable from the browser.**

## Current Infrastructure

- **GCP project**: petprojects-488115
- **VM**: simple-zombie, e2-standard-4, Ubuntu 24.04, europe-west1-b
- **Gateway**: Caddy with auto-HTTPS
- **Auth**: Google OAuth2 (allowed emails configured in `attlas-server-config` secret)
- **Domain**: attlas.uk (Cloudflare DNS, auto-updated on provision)
- **Dashboard**: Go binary (`base-setup/alive-server/main.go`)

## GCP Secret Manager secrets

| Secret | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `github-pat` | GitHub PAT for cloning repos |
| `cloudflare-dns-token` | Cloudflare API token for DNS updates |
| `attlas-server-config` | OAuth2 client ID/secret + allowed emails |
| `openclaw-config` | OpenClaw channel tokens and API keys |

## Diary

The `diary/` folder contains one entry per working session. Each entry logs what was done, lessons learned, and the Claude session ID. **Always update the diary at the end of each session.**

## Git Identity

- Name: commonlisp6
- Email: gcp.vm.clawde@me.com

# services

Browser-accessible services running behind the Caddy gateway.

## Current Services

| Service | Script | Port | URL Path | Description |
|---------|--------|------|----------|-------------|
| terminal | `install-terminal.sh` | 7681 | /terminal | Web terminal (ttyd, zsh with dotfiles) |
| code-server | `install-code-server.sh` | 8080 | /code | VS Code in the browser |
| openclaw | `install-openclaw.sh` | 18789 | /openclaw | AI agent daemon (Telegram, Brave, Anthropic) |
| diary | `install-diary.sh` | — | /diary | Hugo-powered project diary (static site) |

## How install.sh Works

`install.sh` is a menu-based installer. It discovers all `install-*.sh` scripts in this directory and presents them as options. You can install individual services or all at once.

## IMPORTANT: Adding a New Service

Every service that needs to be reachable from the internet **MUST** have a Caddy route snippet. Without it, the service is only accessible on localhost.

To add a new service:

1. Create `install-myservice.sh` — the second comment line becomes the menu description
2. Inside the script:
   - Install the binary (with idempotency guard: `if ! command -v ... &>/dev/null`)
   - Create a systemd unit
   - **Copy a `.caddy` route snippet to `/etc/caddy/conf.d/`**
3. Create `myservice.caddy` in this directory:
   ```caddyfile
   handle /myservice* {
       reverse_proxy localhost:PORT
   }
   ```
4. `install.sh` will auto-discover the new `install-myservice.sh` and show it in the menu
5. Caddy is reloaded automatically after all selected services are installed

## Usage

Run as root:

```bash
sudo bash /home/agnostic-user/iapetus/attlas/services/install.sh
```

## Service user contract

- `install-terminal.sh`, `install-code-server.sh`, `install-diary.sh` default to `SERVICE_USER=agnostic-user` and `User=agnostic-user` in the systemd unit. Browser shells / editors thus land in the login user's home.
- `install-openclaw.sh` defaults to `SERVICE_USER=openclaw-svc` (a nologin system user) with `SERVICE_STATE_DIR=/var/lib/openclaw` and sets `Environment=HOME=${SERVICE_STATE_DIR}` on the unit, so openclaw finds its state at `/var/lib/openclaw/.openclaw/`.
- `alive-server.service` itself is installed by `base-setup/setup.sh` (not here) and runs as the dedicated `alive-svc` user.

85	3920b876-927e-4f43-8690-a73f2a2b2d8e [assistant] Exciting one. I've read through the attlas layout — I won't touch code yet, let me lay out my thinking and then ask you the questions that will actually change the design.
86	
87	## What I think we're building
88	
89	A new service `petboard` that lives in `attlas/services/`, exposed at `attlas.uk/petboard/` behind the same Caddy + OAuth2 gate as the others. Data gets written by Claude Code during planning sessions; the web UI is the visual dashboard you look at.
90	
91	## Recommended shape (to react to, not final)
92	
93	**Three layers:**
94	
95	1. **Data** — plain JSON files in `attlas/services/petboard/data/projects/<slug>.json`, one file per project, git-committed. Human-readable, diff-able, you get a *free* audit log via git history. Schema roughly:
96	   ```
97	   { id, name, priority, color, created_at,
98	     features: [{ id, title, created_at, completed_at?, effort_minutes?, status }],
99	     effort_log: [{ date, minutes, note }] }
100	   ```
101	2. **Web UI** — small Go server (mirrors `base-setup/alive-server`), serves a static HTML + D3.js page. Reads the JSON files, watches them with fsnotify, pushes updates to the browser over SSE. So when Claude edits a file in another terminal, the board animates live.
102	3. **Claude integration** — see below.
103	
104	## UX proposal — the board
105	
106	Main view = **Gantt-style horizontal swimlanes, one per project, ranked by priority top-to-bottom.** X axis is calendar time; there's a vertical "now" line that ticks in real time.
107	
108	For each lane:
109	- **Left gutter**: rank number, project name, color chip, and a compact progress ring — "7/12 done" — plus total effort (e.g. "14h").
110	- **The bar**: stretches from `created_at` to now. The bar itself is a soft muted color; on top of it, two kinds of marks:
111	  - **Open circles** where a feature was *added to the backlog*
112	  - **Filled circles** where a feature was *completed*
113	  - A faint line connects a feature's "added" and "completed" circles so you can see cycle time visually
114	- **Hover** any circle → tooltip with feature title and dates.
115	- **Click a lane** → right-side drawer slides open with the full backlog grouped by status, effort log, and recent history.
116	
117	Extras that are cheap:
118	- Zoom: week / month / quarter toggle.
119	- Drag to reorder lanes = reprioritize (writes back to the JSON).
120	- Dark mode to match ttyd/code-server aesthetic.
121	- Each project color is hashed from its id so it's stable across rebuilds.
122	
123	Why this specifically: the thing you said you care about — "when it started, when features were added, when they were completed, how much is done, how much effort, which is highest priority" — maps one-to-one onto (lane start) + (open circles) + (filled circles) + (progress ring) + (effort label) + (lane order). Everything is visible at a glance without clicking.
124	
125	**References you might want to look at for taste**: linear.app's cycle view, plane.so's timeline, and vis-timeline's Gantt demos. I'd copy the *density* of linear but the *interactivity* of vis-timeline.
126	
127	## Skill vs MCP server — my recommendation
128	
129	Both are viable. Here's the honest tradeoff:
130	
131	|                         | Skill                                                           | MCP server                                                                 |
132	| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
133	| What Claude sees        | Markdown instructions + "edit these JSON files with this schema" | Structured tools: `create_project`, `add_feature`, `complete_feature`, ... |
134	| Setup cost              | ~1 file in `~/.claude/skills/`                                  | Real process, manifest, has to run somewhere                               |
135	| Validation / safety     | Relies on Claude reading schema correctly                        | Enforced by tool signatures                                                |
136	| Discoverable everywhere | Only when user says `/petboard` or mentions it                  | Auto-available in any session configured with the server                    |
137	| Debugging               | Just read the file                                              | Need to check logs of the MCP process                                      |
138	| State                   | Stateless (files are the state)                                  | Same (files are still the state)                                            |
139	
140	**My recommendation: start with a skill, keep an open door for MCP.**
141	
142	Reasons:
143	1. You'll probably plan pet projects from *multiple* contexts (local mac, `/terminal`, `/code`). A skill stored in dotfiles syncs everywhere for free; an MCP server has to be reachable from each of them, which means network plumbing.
144	2. Writes are infrequent (a few per planning session). Schema is small. Claude handles JSON edits reliably when the schema is right in front of it.
145	3. The web UI + the git history already give you strong safety nets — if Claude writes something weird, you see it immediately on the board and in `git diff`.
146	4. If it *does* get painful (e.g. you want concurrency guards, or complex queries), promoting the skill to an MCP server later is mechanical: the tool names you'd define are already the verbs in the skill.
147	
148	The skill would basically say: *"When the user talks about pet projects, read `~/iapetus/attlas/services/petboard/data/projects/*.json`. Here is the schema. Here are the common operations and how to express each as a file edit. After editing, commit with message `petboard: <what changed>`."*
149	
150	## Questions that will actually change the design
151	
152	Before I write a plan doc, these matter:
153	
154	1. **Where does Claude Code run when you're planning?** Local mac only, `/terminal` on the VM only, or both? (Determines whether data lives in the repo on the VM or is synced.)
155	2. **Effort unit** — hours? session count? pomodoros? I'd default to minutes logged per session.
156	3. **Feature statuses** — just `backlog` / `done`, or also `in_progress`, `blocked`, `dropped`?
157	4. **Priority** — explicit numeric rank that you'd re-shuffle, or implicit from lane order (drag-to-reorder)?
158	5. **Completed features** — stay pinned on the timeline forever, or fade/collapse after some time so active work stays visible?
159	6. **Tech taste** — are you OK with a Go backend + vanilla JS/D3 frontend (matches attlas style, no build step), or do you want me to reach for Svelte/Vite? The former is simpler, the latter is nicer to extend.
160	7. **One board for all projects, or per-project deep-dive pages too?** I'd start with the single board only; per-project pages can come later if you miss them.
161	
162	Tell me what resonates and what doesn't, and I'll turn it into a proper plan doc before touching code.

164	876b9570-5b2e-4c12-b419-ae9844dbc024 [assistant] thinking
165	73156cf4-b917-4dd7-9def-0efcb8596383 [assistant] Great answers — this changes the architecture meaningfully. Here's where I land after your choices.
166	
167	## New shape of the service
168	
169	```
170	attlas/services/petboard/
171	├── install-petboard.sh        # installs go binary + sqlite state + caddy route
172	├── uninstall-petboard.sh
173	├── petboard.caddy             # reverse proxy /petboard*
174	├── server/                    # go backend
175	│   ├── main.go
176	│   ├── db/                    # sqlite + embedded migrations
177	│   ├── api/                   # REST handlers + SSE
178	│   └── static/                # embedded built react app
179	└── web/                       # vite + react source
180	    ├── src/
181	    └── package.json
182	```
183	
184	**Single Go binary** that embeds (a) SQLite migrations, (b) the built React bundle, (c) the REST API, (d) an SSE stream for live updates. Same pattern as `base-setup/alive-server` — one binary, one systemd unit, one Caddy snippet.
185	
186	**State**: `/var/lib/petboard/petboard.db`, owned by a new `petboard-svc` nologin user. Mirrors the openclaw pattern.
187	
188	**Backend deps**: `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure-Go, no CGO so the build stays clean), `net/http` only. No chi, no gorm — one-file handlers, raw SQL. Matches attlas taste.
189	
190	**Frontend deps**: React + Vite + TypeScript, `react-konva` for the canvas (React-friendly wrapper around Konva, which is a solid 2D canvas engine with built-in pan/zoom and high node counts), `@tanstack/react-query` for server state, Tailwind for the UI chrome (toolbar, drawer, forms). No state-management library — react-query + local `useState` is enough.
191	
192	Built with `vite build --base=/petboard/` and embedded into the Go binary with `embed.FS`. One artifact to deploy.
193	
194	## The canvas — my pitch
195	
196	You said excalidraw-feel + futuristic + infinite zoom + things appearing at different zoom levels. Here's the concrete design I'd build. I'm still anchoring on time because your original brief wanted "when it started, when features were added, when completed" — but the canvas itself is infinite and pannable, not a locked viewport.
197	
198	**The universe:**
199	- Dark space background with a very subtle grid that scales with zoom.
200	- **X axis = time** (calendar). **Y axis = projects**, but not in a rigid grid — each project's "track" floats at its own Y position and you can drag it.
201	- Each project is a glowing **thread** that starts at its `created_at` and extends to the "now" line. Thread color is derived from the project id. Priority controls glow intensity and thread thickness (High = thick + bright pulse, Medium = normal, Low = thin + dim).
202	- **Features are orbs on the thread**:
203	  - Open ring = added to backlog
204	  - Solid glowing orb = completed
205	  - `in_progress` = pulsing ring
206	  - `dropped` = faint, desaturated ring
207	  - A thin line connects a feature's add-orb to its done-orb so cycle time is visible as distance.
208	- A vertical **"now" line** ticks in real time, with a subtle particle effect.
209	- **Session effort** appears as small tick marks underneath the thread at the times effort was logged — so the thread visually "thickens" in busy periods.
210	
211	**Semantic zoom — the excalidraw feel:**
212	- **Zoomed all the way out**: projects are just colored threads in the void. You see the shape of your life — which projects are long-running, which are new, which are dense with activity. No text visible.
213	- **Mid zoom**: project names appear as floating labels near the start of each thread. Feature orbs become clickable. Progress ring ("7/12") appears next to the project label.
214	- **Zoomed in**: feature orbs expand into small cards showing title + dates. Effort ticks become readable. A mini-legend appears per project.
215	- **Zoomed way in**: feature cards expand into full descriptions; effort log entries show as text annotations under the thread.
216	
217	Konva makes this easy: each layer of detail is its own `Group` with a visibility rule based on the current zoom scalar.
218	
219	**Interactions:**
220	- Mouse wheel = zoom centered on cursor. Drag empty space = pan. Two-finger trackpad works the same.
221	- Drag a project label up/down = re-order (visual only, or persist — your call).
222	- Click a feature orb → side drawer slides in with the feature's details.
223	- Click a project label → **navigates to `/petboard/p/<slug>`**, the project detail page (your "both" answer).
224	- Top-right: a floating toolbar with zoom buttons, a "fit all" button, and a **time-window slider** that fades features outside the window to near-zero opacity (so completed features can stay "forever" but hide on demand, as you asked).
225	- Top-left: priority filter chips (High / Medium / Low) and status filter chips (backlog / in progress / done / dropped).
226	
227	**The project detail page** (`/petboard/p/<slug>`): not on the canvas. Standard React page with:
228	- Header: name, priority, color, created, total effort
229	- Three-column board: Backlog / In Progress / Done (+ a collapsed Dropped section)
230	- Effort log as a sparkline + list
231	- "Add feature" and "Log effort" buttons
232	- Back to the universe
233	
234	Two views, one data model.
235	
236	## Claude Code integration — verdict flipped
237	
238	With data now behind a REST API, the skill-edits-files approach is dead. **MCP is the right answer now.** Concretely:
239	
240	- A tiny second Go binary `petboard-mcp` (or a subcommand of the same binary: `petboard mcp`) that speaks MCP over stdio and wraps the REST API.
241	- Distributed via dotfiles so it's available on your mac and on the VM. Configured per-host in Claude Code's MCP config, with `PETBOARD_URL` + `PETBOARD_TOKEN` env vars.
242	- Tools it exposes:
243	  - `list_projects`, `get_project`
244	  - `create_project(name, priority, color?)`
245	  - `update_project(id, priority?|name?)`
246	  - `add_feature(project_id, title)`
247	  - `set_feature_status(feature_id, status)`
248	  - `log_effort(project_id, minutes, note?)`
249	  - `search(query)` — natural language over project/feature titles
250	
251	- **Plus** a small skill at `dotfiels/claude/skills/petboard.md` that encodes *policy* (naming conventions, when to bump priority, when to log effort, how to phrase feature titles). Skill teaches the *taste*, MCP exposes the *mechanism*.
252	
253	## The auth wrinkle (worth deciding now)
254	
255	Right now `attlas.uk/*` is gated by Google OAuth2. An MCP server can't do OAuth flows. We need to punch a hole for the API:
256	
257	- **Option 1**: Caddy route `/petboard/api/*` accepts `Authorization: Bearer <token>` instead of OAuth. Token stored in Secret Manager as `petboard-api-token`, baked into the petboard server config on install. The Go server validates the bearer.
258	- **Option 2**: Keep API behind OAuth, run the MCP server only on the VM (stdio, localhost API calls). Local-mac Claude sessions can't manage petboard.
259	- **Option 3**: Both — OAuth for the UI, bearer for the API, validated in the Go server itself (not in Caddy), so Caddy just reverse-proxies.
260	
261	I'd do **Option 3**. Caddy stays dumb, the Go server enforces OAuth on HTML/SSE routes and bearer on `/api/*` routes. Clean and single-source-of-truth for auth.
262	
263	## Questions before I write the plan doc
264	
265	1. **Auth**: OK with option 3 above — a new `petboard-api-token` in Secret Manager, separate from OAuth for `/api/*`?
266	2. **Y-layout of the canvas**: do you want project thread positions **persisted** (drag to arrange your universe, and it stays that way), or **auto-laid-out** each session (algorithmic, sorted by priority)? I'd default to persisted because that's more excalidraw-y.
267	3. **Konva vs tldraw vs custom**: I'm recommending `react-konva`. If you have a strong pull toward something like `tldraw` (it *is* essentially excalidraw-for-react), I can go that route instead — but tldraw is opinionated about being a whiteboard, so bending it to a timeline is more work. `react-konva` gives us a blank WebGL-ish canvas and we paint whatever we want.
268	4. **Single-user assumption**: this is just for you, right? No multi-user story? (Keeps the schema and auth simple.)
269	5. **Time on the X-axis — linear or log?** Linear is expected, but log (more pixels for recent time, less for old time) would mean zooming out still leaves recent work readable. Optional fanciness.
270	6. **"Both" for Claude Code** — confirming: you want the MCP server available when Claude Code runs locally on your mac AND when it runs inside `/terminal` or `/code` on the VM. Both cases point at `https://attlas.uk/petboard/api` with the bearer token. Correct?
271	7. **Hot reload during dev**: happy with `vite dev` pointing at the Go server via a proxy, and `go run` with a file watcher? Or do you want me to skip dev ergonomics and just `vite build` on every change?
272	
273	Tell me what clicks and what doesn't on the canvas design especially — that's the most opinionated part of this and I'd rather iterate on it before writing code.

275	40477358-0660-4a2f-9245-17fc6f97fe55 [assistant] thinking
276	1dca80e1-2f79-4c58-bc36-b1787bd4c0e7 [assistant] You're right, and my original statement was sloppy. Let me correct it and walk through what's actually possible.
277	
278	## What I got wrong
279	
280	The MCP spec supports OAuth 2.1 authorization, and Claude Code supports remote MCP servers over HTTP with custom headers (including `Cookie`). So an MCP server absolutely *can* sit behind an auth layer. My earlier framing collapsed two things that aren't the same:
281	
282	1. **MCP transport choice** — stdio (local subprocess) vs streamable HTTP (network endpoint)
283	2. **Who does auth** — local trust vs. a real auth gate
284	
285	I jumped straight to "stdio local process with a bearer token" without considering that you can serve MCP *as an HTTP route in the Go server*, which would then inherit whatever Caddy already enforces.
286	
287	## What you're proposing, spelled out
288	
289	```
290	                  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
291	                  │                Caddy                    │
292	                  │  (Google OAuth2 → sets session cookie)  │
293	                  └────────────────────┬────────────────────┘
294	                                       │ validated request
295	                                       ▼
296	                  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
297	                  │               petboard                  │
298	                  │  /          → React static site         │
299	                  │  /api/*     → REST                      │
300	                  │  /events    → SSE                       │
301	                  │  /mcp       → streamable HTTP MCP  ◄──── │
302	                  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
303	```
304	
305	Caddy is the auth gate. The Go server serves `/petboard/mcp` as an MCP HTTP endpoint. Claude Code is configured as a remote MCP client pointing at `https://attlas.uk/petboard/mcp`, presenting the Caddy session cookie on each request.
306	
307	**This works.** Concretely:
308	
309	- Caddy's OAuth2 plugin validates the cookie and forwards the request to the Go server with headers like `X-Forwarded-User`. The Go server just trusts the upstream.
310	- Claude Code supports remote MCP with header customization — you register the server with a `Cookie: <caddy-session>` header.
311	- Same auth boundary for the web UI and for Claude's tool calls. No second secret, no second auth system, no bearer-token bypass to worry about.
312	
313	## The real tradeoff
314	
315	The question becomes: **how does the cookie get into Claude Code's MCP config, and what happens when it expires?**
316	
317	Three variants, in order of elegance:
318	
319	**B1 — Manual cookie paste**
320	- You log in to `attlas.uk` in your browser (which you already do). Copy the session cookie from devtools once. Paste it into Claude Code's MCP config as a header. Re-paste whenever it expires.
321	- Zero code. Real friction every few days depending on Caddy's session TTL.
322	
323	**B2 — Helper CLI `petboard auth login`**
324	- A subcommand of the petboard binary. It starts a local callback server on `127.0.0.1:<port>`, opens your browser to `attlas.uk/petboard/auth/login?callback=...`, the Go server (after Caddy has validated the OAuth cookie) redirects back to localhost with the cookie value, CLI writes it into Claude Code's MCP config.
325	- Same UX as `gcloud auth login`. Run it once per session expiry.
326	- Modest code. Clean.
327	
328	**B3 — Real MCP OAuth 2.1**
329	- Go server implements the MCP authorization server metadata endpoints (`/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server`, `/authorize`, `/token`) and federates to Google OAuth under the hood. Claude Code sees the MCP server needs auth, drives the flow end-to-end with no helper CLI.
330	- Most correct, most code. Overkill for a single-user pet project but future-proof.
331	
332	My updated recommendation: **B2**. It reuses your existing Caddy OAuth (no duplicate auth system), gives you a proper `login` command so there's nothing manual to copy around, and the code is maybe 80 lines of Go. When the cookie expires, you type `petboard auth login`, browser opens, you click once, done.
333	
334	## What changes in the service layout
335	
336	With MCP inside the Go binary, we drop the separate `petboard-mcp` binary entirely. The Go server gains a `/mcp` handler and the subcommand `petboard auth login`. The web UI, REST API, SSE stream, and MCP endpoint all live in one binary, one systemd unit, one Caddy snippet. Caddy already has `/petboard*` behind OAuth2, so nothing changes on the Caddy side.
337	
338	The skill at `dotfiels/claude/skills/petboard.md` still earns its keep — it tells Claude *how to use the tools* (naming conventions, when to bump priority, how to phrase feature titles), not *how to authenticate*.
339	
340	## One wrinkle to flag
341	
342	Cookie forwarding works cleanly as long as Caddy's session cookie is `SameSite=Lax` or weaker on the paths Claude Code hits. If caddy-security defaults to `SameSite=Strict` and scopes the cookie to `attlas.uk`, Claude Code — running as a non-browser client with a custom `Cookie` header — should still be fine because SameSite only applies to browser-originated requests. But it's worth verifying on the VM before I commit to this, because if the cookie turns out to be HttpOnly + tied to some browser-fingerprint, we fall back to B3 (real OAuth) or the bearer-token plan.
343	
344	I want to **test this on your VM** before locking it into the plan — I'll log in, grab the cookie, hit `/petboard/` (or any existing authed route) with `curl` and the `Cookie:` header, and verify Caddy accepts it. If that works end-to-end, B2 is the plan.
345	
346	Does this framing match what you were getting at? And are you good with me doing that cookie probe against the live VM before we finalize the design, or would you rather we commit to one of the paths now and discover issues during implementation?

352

Subcommand `petboard auth login` that: spawns a localhost listener on a random port → opens your browser to `https://attlas.uk/petboard/auth/cli?port=<n>` → after Caddy validates the OAuth cookie, the Go server's `/auth/cli` handler 302's back to `http://127.0.0.1:<n>/callback?cookie=<value>` → CLI captures it and writes it to Claude Code's MCP config at `~/.claude.json` (or wherever the `mcpServers` live) under the petboard entry's `headers.Cookie` field.

366	
367	Once these are settled I'll write the plan doc (probably at `attlas/services/petboard/PLAN.md`) with the full schema, REST surface, MCP tool list, canvas interaction spec, and the install script outline — then you review that before any code gets written.

7f4554fe-fc33-472c-b97d-ca62bd21b30e [user] Hi, i am about to make a plan for a pet project. BUt the plan itself is quite big. I would like for us to first create a nice system to create such plans and need some help from you also with design suggestions. But i want it to be very visual. LEts call this the "pet project management tool". You can add it as a new service. I want to have an interective 2d board that shows the name of the project and that it lands in some sort of interactive timeline, basically i want to have a timeline view that shows when a pet proejct started and when features were added to the backlog and when features were completed, i think 2d is fine as long as its cool and interactive.  It should show how much features are done and open in the backlog for each project, and how much effort was spent on the pet project so far, there should also be a way of ranking which one has the most prioritiy. Maybe a swiming lane for each project, i am not usre i am not so good thinking of UX.  It should be possible to add new projects and to easily add new features to the backlog as well as saying when they are completed. Now very important. We will create this plans for pet proejcts with claude code, which means we will talk about new projects like bbuilding a home labd with raspebery pis and you will need to create and manage the pet proejct and their features. I think this means we need to turn the pet project management tool into an mpc server or maybe even creating a skill for claude code, i need some help here.  Lets talk about it before you start implementing anything. Im very excited about this one, but i will need your help with UX and UI suggestions Claude!

## UX proposal — the board

Main view = **Gantt-style horizontal swimlanes, one per project, ranked by priority top-to-bottom.** X axis is calendar time; there's a vertical "now" line that ticks in real time.

For each lane:
- **Left gutter**: rank number, project name, color chip, and a compact progress ring — "7/12 done" — plus total effort (e.g. "14h").
- **The bar**: stretches from `created_at` to now. The bar itself is a soft muted color; on top of it, two kinds of marks:
  - **Open circles** where a feature was *added to the backlog*
  - **Filled circles** where a feature was *completed*
  - A faint line connects a feature's "added" and "completed" circles so you can see cycle time visually
- **Hover** any circle → tooltip with feature title and dates.
- **Click a lane** → right-side drawer slides open with the full backlog grouped by status, effort log, and recent history.

Extras that are cheap:
- Zoom: week / month / quarter toggle.
- Drag to reorder lanes = reprioritize (writes back to the JSON).
- Dark mode to match ttyd/code-server aesthetic.
- Each project color is hashed from its id so it's stable across rebuilds.

Why this specifically: the thing you said you care about — "when it started, when features were added, when they were completed, how much is done, how much effort, which is highest priority" — maps one-to-one onto (lane start) + (open circles) + (filled circles) + (progress ring) + (effort label) + (lane order). Everything is visible at a glance without clicking.

**References you might want to look at for taste**: linear.app's cycle view, plane.so's timeline, and vis-timeline's Gantt demos. I'd copy the *density* of linear but the *interactivity* of vis-timeline.

## Skill vs MCP server — my recommendation

Both are viable. Here's the honest tradeoff:

|                         | Skill                                                           | MCP server                                                                 |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| What Claude sees        | Markdown instructions + "edit these JSON files with this schema" | Structured tools: `create_project`, `add_feature`, `complete_feature`, ... |
| Setup cost              | ~1 file in `~/.claude/skills/`                                  | Real process, manifest, has to run somewhere                               |
| Validation / safety     | Relies on Claude reading schema correctly                        | Enforced by tool signatures                                                |
| Discoverable everywhere | Only when user says `/petboard` or mentions it                  | Auto-available in any session configured with the server                    |
| Debugging               | Just read the file                                              | Need to check logs of the MCP process                                      |
| State                   | Stateless (files are the state)                                  | Same (files are still the state)                                            |

**My recommendation: start with a skill, keep an open door for MCP.**

Reasons:
1. You'll probably plan pet projects from *multiple* contexts (local mac, `/terminal`, `/code`). A skill stored in dotfiles syncs everywhere for free; an MCP server has to be reachable from each of them, which means network plumbing.
2. Writes are infrequent (a few per planning session). Schema is small. Claude handles JSON edits reliably when the schema is right in front of it.
3. The web UI + the git history already give you strong safety nets — if Claude writes something weird, you see it immediately on the board and in `git diff`.
4. If it *does* get painful (e.g. you want concurrency guards, or complex queries), promoting the skill to an MCP server later is mechanical: the tool names you'd define are already the verbs in the skill.

The skill would basically say: *"When the user talks about pet projects, read `~/iapetus/attlas/services/petboard/data/projects/*.json`. Here is the schema. Here are the common operations and how to express each as a file edit. After editing, commit with message `petboard: <what changed>`."*

## New shape of the service

```
attlas/services/petboard/

├── server/                    # go backend
│   ├── main.go

│   └── static/                # embedded built react app
└── web/                       # vite + react source
    ├── src/
    └── package.json
```

**Single Go binary** that embeds (a) SQLite migrations, (b) the built React bundle, (c) the REST API, (d) an SSE stream for live updates. Same pattern as `base-setup/alive-server` — one binary, one systemd unit, one Caddy snippet.

**State**: `/var/lib/petboard/petboard.db`, owned by a new `petboard-svc` nologin user. Mirrors the openclaw pattern.

**Backend deps**: `modernc.org/sqlite` (pure-Go, no CGO so the build stays clean), `net/http` only. No chi, no gorm — one-file handlers, raw SQL. Matches attlas taste.

**Frontend deps**: React + Vite + TypeScript, `react-konva` for the canvas (React-friendly wrapper around Konva, which is a solid 2D canvas engine with built-in pan/zoom and high node counts), `@tanstack/react-query` for server state, Tailwind for the UI chrome (toolbar, drawer, forms). No state-management library — react-query + local `useState` is enough.

Built with `vite build --base=/petboard/` and embedded into the Go binary with `embed.FS`. One artifact to deploy.

## The canvas — my pitch

You said excalidraw-feel + futuristic + infinite zoom + things appearing at different zoom levels. Here's the concrete design I'd build. I'm still anchoring on time because your original brief wanted "when it started, when features were added, when completed" — but the canvas itself is infinite and pannable, not a locked viewport.

**The universe:**
- Dark space background with a very subtle grid that scales with zoom.
- **X axis = time** (calendar). **Y axis = projects**, but not in a rigid grid — each project's "track" floats at its own Y position and you can drag it.
- Each project is a glowing **thread** that starts at its `created_at` and extends to the "now" line. Thread color is derived from the project id. Priority controls glow intensity and thread thickness (High = thick + bright pulse, Medium = normal, Low = thin + dim).
- **Features are orbs on the thread**:
  - Open ring = added to backlog
  - Solid glowing orb = completed
  - `in_progress` = pulsing ring
  - `dropped` = faint, desaturated ring
  - A thin line connects a feature's add-orb to its done-orb so cycle time is visible as distance.
- A vertical **"now" line** ticks in real time, with a subtle particle effect.
- **Session effort** appears as small tick marks underneath the thread at the times effort was logged — so the thread visually "thickens" in busy periods.

**Semantic zoom — the excalidraw feel:**
- **Zoomed all the way out**: projects are just colored threads in the void. You see the shape of your life — which projects are long-running, which are new, which are dense with activity. No text visible.
- **Mid zoom**: project names appear as floating labels near the start of each thread. Feature orbs become clickable. Progress ring ("7/12") appears next to the project label.
- **Zoomed in**: feature orbs expand into small cards showing title + dates. Effort ticks become readable. A mini-legend appears per project.
- **Zoomed way in**: feature cards expand into full descriptions; effort log entries show as text annotations under the thread.

Konva makes this easy: each layer of detail is its own `Group` with a visibility rule based on the current zoom scalar.

**Interactions:**
- Mouse wheel = zoom centered on cursor. Drag empty space = pan. Two-finger trackpad works the same.
- Drag a project label up/down = re-order (visual only, or persist — your call).
- Click a feature orb → side drawer slides in with the feature's details.
- Click a project label → **navigates to `/petboard/p/<slug>`**, the project detail page (your "both" answer).
- Top-right: a floating toolbar with zoom buttons, a "fit all" button, and a **time-window slider** that fades features outside the window to near-zero opacity (so completed features can stay "forever" but hide on demand, as you asked).
- Top-left: priority filter chips (High / Medium / Low) and status filter chips (backlog / in progress / done / dropped).

**The project detail page** (`/petboard/p/<slug>`): not on the canvas. Standard React page with:
- Header: name, priority, color, created, total effort
- Three-column board: Backlog / In Progress / Done (+ a collapsed Dropped section)
- Effort log as a sparkline + list
- "Add feature" and "Log effort" buttons
- Back to the universe

Two views, one data model.

## Claude Code integration — verdict flipped

With data now behind a REST API, the skill-edits-files approach is dead. **MCP is the right answer now.** Concretely:

- A tiny second Go binary `petboard-mcp` (or a subcommand of the same binary: `petboard mcp`) that speaks MCP over stdio and wraps the REST API.
- Distributed via dotfiles so it's available on your mac and on the VM. Configured per-host in Claude Code's MCP config, with `PETBOARD_URL` + `PETBOARD_TOKEN` env vars.
- Tools it exposes:
  - `list_projects`, `get_project`
  - `create_project(name, priority, color?)`
  - `update_project(id, priority?|name?)`
  - `add_feature(project_id, title)`
  - `set_feature_status(feature_id, status)`
  - `log_effort(project_id, minutes, note?)`
  - `search(query)` — natural language over project/feature titles

- **Plus** a small skill at `dotfiels/claude/skills/petboard.md` that encodes *policy* (naming conventions, when to bump priority, when to log effort, how to phrase feature titles). Skill teaches the *taste*, MCP exposes the *mechanism*.

## The auth wrinkle (worth deciding now)

Right now `attlas.uk/*` is gated by Google OAuth2. An MCP server can't do OAuth flows. We need to punch a hole for the API:

- **Option 1**: Caddy route `/petboard/api/*` accepts `Authorization: Bearer <token>` instead of OAuth. Token stored in Secret Manager as `petboard-api-token`, baked into the petboard server config on install. The Go server validates the bearer.
- **Option 2**: Keep API behind OAuth, run the MCP server only on the VM (stdio, localhost API calls). Local-mac Claude sessions can't manage petboard.
- **Option 3**: Both — OAuth for the UI, bearer for the API, validated in the Go server itself (not in Caddy), so Caddy just reverse-proxies.

I'd do **Option 3**. Caddy stays dumb, the Go server enforces OAuth on HTML/SSE routes and bearer on `/api/*` routes. Clean and single-source-of-truth for auth.

## What I got wrong

The MCP spec supports OAuth 2.1 authorization, and Claude Code supports remote MCP servers over HTTP with custom headers (including `Cookie`). So an MCP server absolutely *can* sit behind an auth layer. My earlier framing collapsed two things that aren't the same:

1. **MCP transport choice** — stdio (local subprocess) vs streamable HTTP (network endpoint)
2. **Who does auth** — local trust vs. a real auth gate

I jumped straight to "stdio local process with a bearer token" without considering that you can serve MCP *as an HTTP route in the Go server*, which would then inherit whatever Caddy already enforces.

## What you're proposing, spelled out

```
                  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │                Caddy                    │
                  │  (Google OAuth2 → sets session cookie)  │
                  └────────────────────┬────────────────────┘
                                       │ validated request
                                       ▼
                  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
                  │               petboard                  │
                  │  /          → React static site         │
                  │  /api/*     → REST                      │
                  │  /events    → SSE                       │
                  │  /mcp       → streamable HTTP MCP  ◄──── │
                  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Caddy is the auth gate. The Go server serves `/petboard/mcp` as an MCP HTTP endpoint. Claude Code is configured as a remote MCP client pointing at `https://attlas.uk/petboard/mcp`, presenting the Caddy session cookie on each request.

**This works.** Concretely:

- Caddy's OAuth2 plugin validates the cookie and forwards the request to the Go server with headers like `X-Forwarded-User`. The Go server just trusts the upstream.
- Claude Code supports remote MCP with header customization — you register the server with a `Cookie: <caddy-session>` header.
- Same auth boundary for the web UI and for Claude's tool calls. No second secret, no second auth system, no bearer-token bypass to worry about.

## The real tradeoff

The question becomes: **how does the cookie get into Claude Code's MCP config, and what happens when it expires?**

Three variants, in order of elegance:

**B1 — Manual cookie paste**
- You log in to `attlas.uk` in your browser (which you already do). Copy the session cookie from devtools once. Paste it into Claude Code's MCP config as a header. Re-paste whenever it expires.
- Zero code. Real friction every few days depending on Caddy's session TTL.

**B2 — Helper CLI `petboard auth login`**
- A subcommand of the petboard binary. It starts a local callback server on `127.0.0.1:<port>`, opens your browser to `attlas.uk/petboard/auth/login?callback=...`, the Go server (after Caddy has validated the OAuth cookie) redirects back to localhost with the cookie value, CLI writes it into Claude Code's MCP config.
- Same UX as `gcloud auth login`. Run it once per session expiry.
- Modest code. Clean.

**B3 — Real MCP OAuth 2.1**
- Go server implements the MCP authorization server metadata endpoints (`/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server`, `/authorize`, `/token`) and federates to Google OAuth under the hood. Claude Code sees the MCP server needs auth, drives the flow end-to-end with no helper CLI.
- Most correct, most code. Overkill for a single-user pet project but future-proof.

My updated recommendation: **B2**. It reuses your existing Caddy OAuth (no duplicate auth system), gives you a proper `login` command so there's nothing manual to copy around, and the code is maybe 80 lines of Go. When the cookie expires, you type `petboard auth login`, browser opens, you click once, done.

## What changes in the service layout

With MCP inside the Go binary, we drop the separate `petboard-mcp` binary entirely. The Go server gains a `/mcp` handler and the subcommand `petboard auth login`. The web UI, REST API, SSE stream, and MCP endpoint all live in one binary, one systemd unit, one Caddy snippet. Caddy already has `/petboard*` behind OAuth2, so nothing changes on the Caddy side.

The skill at `dotfiels/claude/skills/petboard.md` still earns its keep — it tells Claude *how to use the tools* (naming conventions, when to bump priority, how to phrase feature titles), not *how to authenticate*.

## One wrinkle to flag

Cookie forwarding works cleanly as long as Caddy's session cookie is `SameSite=Lax` or weaker on the paths Claude Code hits. If caddy-security defaults to `SameSite=Strict` and scopes the cookie to `attlas.uk`, Claude Code — running as a non-browser client with a custom `Cookie` header — should still be fine because SameSite only applies to browser-originated requests. But it's worth verifying on the VM before I commit to this, because if the cookie turns out to be HttpOnly + tied to some browser-fingerprint, we fall back to B3 (real OAuth) or the bearer-token plan.

I want to **test this on your VM** before locking it into the plan — I'll log in, grab the cookie, hit `/petboard/` (or any existing authed route) with `curl` and the `Cookie:` header, and verify Caddy accepts it. If that works end-to-end, B2 is the plan.

Does this framing match what you were getting at? And are you good with me doing that cookie probe against the live VM before we finalize the design, or would you rather we commit to one of the paths now and discover issues during implementation?

**Schema**: `problem TEXT NOT NULL CHECK (length(trim(problem)) > 0)` on `projects`. Editable after creation (you'll want to refine the framing as you learn), but never nullable.

**API/MCP**: `create_project` requires `problem`. `update_project` accepts `problem?`. Server validates non-empty + trimmed.

**UI placement**:
- Project detail page: prominent block right under the name, styled like a pull quote
- Canvas: shown in a tooltip on hover of the project label, and in the side drawer when you click
- Universe overview is about *when*, detail page is about *why* — but the problem is important enough that it leaks into hover

400	
401	**Skill guidance** (the important part, since Claude writes these). The skill should say something like:
402	
403	> A `problem` is a **one-paragraph framing of the real-world pain the project addresses**. It is not a description of the solution. Good problem statements:
404	> - Name the person or situation feeling the pain
405	> - Describe the current workaround or lack thereof
406	> - Explain why the pain is worth solving right now, not later
407	> - Avoid naming the implementation ("we'll use Raspberry Pis" belongs in `description`, not `problem`)
408	>
409	> Template: *"When [situation], [person] needs [outcome] because [reason/cost of not having it]. Today they [current hacky workaround or nothing]."*
410	>
411	> Examples:
412	> - **Good**: "When I'm debugging a flaky service on the VM, I don't have a fast way to correlate logs across the three systemd units. Today I `journalctl -u ...` in three panes and eyeball timestamps, which is slow and error-prone."
413	> - **Bad**: "Build a log aggregator with OpenSearch and Grafana." (That's the solution.)
414	> - **Bad**: "Logs are annoying." (No situation, no person, no cost.)
415	>
416	> Before calling `create_project`, draft the problem statement, read it back to the user, and only call the tool once the user approves.
417	
418	A couple of questions on this:
419	1. **Minimum length** — should we enforce one? I'd start with "trimmed non-empty" only and rely on the skill + your review. Easy to add later.
420	2. **Show the problem on the canvas overview?** I proposed hover-tooltip only. Or: at max zoom the full problem renders under the project label?
421	3. **User approval before create** — the skill sketch above says Claude reads the problem back and waits for OK. Sound right, or do you want it silent?
422	
423	## Change 2 — proper MCP OAuth 2.1 flow
424	
425	Yes, this is the right call. I dug into the base Caddyfile and there's a nicer path than I first thought — we can do real OAuth 2.1 **without duplicating Google OAuth in the Go server**.
426	
427	### The base Caddyfile reality
428	
429	```
430	@protected {
431	    not path /oauth2/* /logout
432	    not path_regexp ^/.*favicon\.(ico|svg|png)$
433	}
434	forward_auth @protected localhost:3000 { uri /api/auth/verify ... }
435	```
436	
437	Everything except `/oauth2/*`, `/logout`, and favicon goes through `alive-server`'s auth. If you're not authed, alive-server redirects you to Google OAuth, sets a session cookie, sends you back to the original URL.
438	
439	That existing Google OAuth is **exactly** what we need for the browser-facing part of MCP OAuth. We shouldn't re-implement it in petboard.
440	
441	### Hybrid architecture
442	
443	Petboard implements only the MCP-specific OAuth 2.1 endpoints. The Google OAuth part is inherited from Caddy+alive-server.
444	
445	Routes and their auth posture:
446	
447	| path                                            | Caddy auth | who handles auth                                        |
448	| ----------------------------------------------- | ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
449	| `/petboard/` (UI)                               | yes        | Caddy → alive-server → Google (existing)                |
450	| `/petboard/api/*`                               | yes        | same                                                    |
451	| `/petboard/events` (SSE)                        | yes        | same                                                    |
452	| `/petboard/oauth/authorize`                     | **yes**    | same — we *want* Caddy auth here (reuse browser session) |
453	| `/petboard/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` | no       | public metadata                                         |
454	| `/petboard/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource`   | no       | public metadata                                         |
455	| `/petboard/oauth/register`                      | no         | dynamic client registration (RFC 7591)                  |
456	| `/petboard/oauth/token`                         | no         | petboard validates PKCE + auth code                     |
457	| `/petboard/mcp`                                 | no         | petboard validates Bearer access token                  |
458	
459	The **one base Caddyfile change** this needs is to extend the `@protected` matcher so those public paths bypass forward_auth:
460	
461	```caddyfile
462	@protected {
463	    not path /oauth2/* /logout
464	    not path /petboard/.well-known/* /petboard/oauth/register /petboard/oauth/token /petboard/mcp
465	    not path_regexp ^/.*favicon\.(ico|svg|png)$
466	}
467	```
468	
469	I know `base-setup/CLAUDE.md` says *"don't add service routes to the base Caddyfile"*, but this isn't a route — it's an auth exemption that a new service genuinely needs. I'll flag it as a deliberate exception and document the rationale in the petboard install script.
470	
471	### End-to-end flow
472	
473	1. Claude Code connects to `https://attlas.uk/petboard/mcp` → 401 with `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer resource_metadata="/petboard/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource"`
474	2. Claude fetches the well-known docs → learns about petboard's auth endpoints
475	3. Claude POSTs `/petboard/oauth/register` → gets a `client_id`
476	4. Claude builds an `/authorize?response_type=code&client_id=...&code_challenge=...&redirect_uri=http://127.0.0.1:<port>/callback&...` URL and opens your default browser
477	5. Caddy sees no session on `/petboard/oauth/authorize` → redirects to Google OAuth → Google → alive-server sets session → back to `/petboard/oauth/authorize?...` with session cookie
478	6. Petboard's `/authorize` handler generates an auth code, stores `(code, code_challenge, client_id)` in SQLite, 302's to `http://127.0.0.1:<port>/callback?code=...`
479	7. Claude Code's local listener captures the code, POSTs `/petboard/oauth/token` with code + PKCE verifier → petboard validates, mints an opaque access token, stores it, returns it
480	8. Claude Code uses `Authorization: Bearer <token>` on all subsequent `/petboard/mcp` calls. Petboard validates the token against SQLite on each request.
481	
482	**No `petboard auth login` CLI**. No cookie copying. Claude Code drives the whole flow — exactly what you wanted. The only thing you do is click through a Google login screen once (or not even that, if Caddy's session is still fresh).
483	
484	### What this adds to the implementation
485	
486	- A small `oauth/` package in the Go server — ~250-400 lines total covering: DCR endpoint, authorize endpoint, token endpoint, PKCE validation, well-known metadata, bearer middleware.
487	- New tables in SQLite: `oauth_clients`, `oauth_auth_codes`, `oauth_access_tokens`.
488	- No external library — hand-rolled minimal OAuth 2.1. Fosite is overkill for a single-user service and drags in a pile of dependencies.
489	- One Caddyfile change in `base-setup/Caddyfile` (documented as a deliberate exception).
490	
491	### Questions before I write the plan
492	
493	1. **Caddyfile change** — OK with me editing `base-setup/Caddyfile` to add the petboard path exemptions? It's the one place I can't avoid touching outside of `services/petboard/`. Alternative is implementing Google OAuth a second time inside petboard, which is way more code.
494	2. **Token lifetime** — access tokens expire? I'd default to **no expiry** for a single-user personal tool, revocable via a `DELETE /api/oauth/tokens/:id` admin endpoint. Standard is 1 hour + refresh tokens, but that's extra complexity for you specifically.
495	3. **Problem field — answers to the three sub-questions above** (min length, canvas visibility, user-approval-before-create)?
496	
497	Once those three land I'll write the updated plan doc.

504	## Why petboard alone can't handle this
505	
506	Caddy is the TLS terminator and the first thing every request hits. The base Caddyfile says: "for everything except `/oauth2/*`, `/logout`, and favicons, do a `forward_auth` subrequest to alive-server." Alive-server checks the session cookie and, if absent, returns a 302 redirect to Google OAuth.
507	
508	```
509	[client] → Caddy → forward_auth → alive-server (/api/auth/verify)
510	                                  ↓
511	                        200 OK            302 to Google
512	                          ↓                     ↓
513	                        petboard         client sees redirect
514	```
515	
516	If Claude Code hits `/petboard/mcp` with a Bearer token, the request is checked by **alive-server before petboard ever sees it**. Alive-server has no session cookie, returns 302 to Google, Claude gets a redirect instead of a 401. MCP auth breaks.
517	
518	Petboard runs *after* this gate. There is literally no code we can write inside the petboard binary that will stop alive-server from being asked first. The only way to make petboard see those requests is to tell the layer in front of it "skip auth for these paths."
519	
520	That layer is either Caddy or alive-server. Not petboard.
521	
522	## Your real options

554	
555	## The move
556	
557	Caddy's `forward_auth` automatically sends `X-Forwarded-Uri` with the original request path to alive-server on every auth check. Alive-server can read that header and decide whether the path needs auth. **No Caddyfile change, ever.**
558	
559	Two variants:
560	
561	### V1 — hardcoded petboard paths in alive-server (smallest)
562	
563	~15 lines added to `handleAuthVerify`:
564	
565	```go
566	var publicPaths = []string{
567	    "/petboard/mcp",
568	    "/petboard/.well-known/",
569	    "/petboard/oauth/register",
570	    "/petboard/oauth/token",
571	}
572	
573	func handleAuthVerify(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
574	    origURI := r.Header.Get("X-Forwarded-Uri")
575	    for _, p := range publicPaths {
576	        if strings.HasPrefix(origURI, p) {
577	            w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
578	            return
579	        }
580	    }
581	    if isAuthenticated(r) {
582	        w.WriteHeader(http.StatusOK)
583	    } else {
584	        http.Redirect(w, r, "/oauth2/login", http.StatusFound)
585	    }
586	}
587	```
588	
589	Simple. Couples alive-server to petboard, but realistically there's only ever going to be one MCP-serving service and the change is tiny.
590	
591	### V2 — generic public-path registry (cleaner, slightly more code)
592	
593	Alive-server reads `/etc/attlas-public-paths.d/*.conf` on each verify (with a small in-memory cache + file watcher). Each line is a path prefix. Any service that needs unauthenticated paths drops its own file during install:
594	
595	```
596	# /etc/attlas-public-paths.d/petboard.conf
597	/petboard/mcp
598	/petboard/.well-known/
599	/petboard/oauth/register
600	/petboard/oauth/token
601	```
602	
603	Alive-server becomes a proper auth gate with a public-path registry. Future services (MCP or otherwise) register themselves cleanly. About ~40 lines in alive-server, no coupling to petboard, good for attlas as a whole.
604	
605	**My recommendation: V2**. Same effect for your current use case, and if attlas ever grows another public-path-needing service, it's already solved.
606	
607	## How `/authorize` still works
608	
609	The authorize endpoint stays protected. Flow:
610	
611	1. Claude Code opens browser to `/petboard/oauth/authorize?...`
612	2. Caddy → alive-server verify → path is NOT in public list → check session cookie
613	3. No session → 302 to `/oauth2/login` → Google → `/oauth2/callback` → session cookie set → redirect back to `/petboard/oauth/authorize?...`
614	4. Re-request now passes alive-server verify → reaches petboard's authorize handler
615	5. Petboard generates auth code, 302s to Claude's local callback
616	6. Claude exchanges code at `/petboard/oauth/token` (public path → straight to petboard), gets access token
617	7. Claude calls `/petboard/mcp` with Bearer token (public path → straight to petboard → petboard validates token)
618	
619	So the user-session part reuses Caddy+alive-server's existing Google OAuth (no duplication), and only the MCP-specific OAuth 2.1 endpoints run through petboard's own code. Claude Code drives the whole flow — no `petboard auth login`, no cookie copying.
620	
621	## The other locked-in answers
622	
623	- **Problem field**: no min length ✓, tooltip + full-zoom rendering under the project label ✓, Claude reads back and waits for user approval before `create_project` ✓.
624	- **Token lifetime**: 30 days, sliding window on use. Refresh tokens add complexity I'd skip; when a token expires, Claude Code just runs the OAuth flow again (one browser click if the Caddy session is still fresh, two if you're fully logged out). Stored in SQLite with `(token_hash, created_at, last_used_at, expires_at)`.
625	- **User approval before create**: confirmed ✓ (I'm reading the cut-off "3." as a confirmation — let me know if it was meant to be something else).
626	
627	## One question to lock in before the plan doc
628	
629	**V1 or V2** for the alive-server change? V1 is 15 lines + zero generality. V2 is ~40 lines + a reusable registry. Both keep base Caddyfile untouched and keep MCP auth fully in Go-land as you asked.
